title: The Possibility Of A Pivot
text:
The Possibility Of A Pivot

Andrew Batson

Gavekal Dragonomics | 24 Jul 2026

With GDP growth disappointing as the oil shock bites, and equity indexes flat or down for the year, debate is once again raging over just how much China’s government will do to support the economy. Domestically, there is discussion about the possibility of another policy “pivot” like that in September 2024, which saw a coordinated effort from multiple agencies to stabilize markets and restore confidence in the outlook. This week, the unusual public messaging from securities regulators and coordinated stock purchases by state institutions did show a serious official effort to support equity prices. But big new stimulus measures are still unlikely, as the current situation differs from the lead-up to the 2024 pivot in a few important ways.One, the current shock to growth is mainly an external one that could well prove temporary. National Bureau of Statistics spokesman Mao Shengyong presented the official view, blaming the slowdown to 4.3% real GDP growth in Q2 on “short-term factors and external influences,” particularly in the energy sector, while “other industries operated normally.” Since the Iran war began, China’s leadership, like most financial-market participants, seems to have assumed that the disruption to global oil supplies is too severe to be sustained, and that the US and Iran would eventually find it in their interests to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
